fields.name: 10. World Day for Cultural Diversity for Dialogue and Development as a National Day
fields.content-01:
Preparation

‍

The teacher should assign the debate topic one week before the debate and provide students with material: handouts on the rules of the debate and sources for research (reputable websites, video materials, presentations). All students should prepare for debate. In this way, the participation of all is enabled, including those who are not direct participants but contribute to the success of the debate. If the preparation takes place in class, it is necessary to provide printed material for students and more time, preferably two school classes (90 minutes).

‍

1. Introduction to the topic (5 minutes)

‍

The teacher starts the lesson by introducing the World Day of Cultural Diversity for Dialogue and Development (May 21) and explains its purpose. Celebrating this day promotes cultural diversity, encourages dialogue between cultures and supports development through cultural exchange, which is recognized by UNESCO.

The teacher asks the question: "Should World Cultural Diversity Day be a national holiday?" Additional question explanation:

"How can cultural diversity benefit a nation?"

"What is the impact of national holidays and why do countries choose to celebrate certain days?"

This introduction may include video material.

‍

2. Forming debate teams (5 minutes)

‍

  • Teacher can divide the class into two teams by random assignment or by allowing students to choose sides:

A (Pro-Team, three speakers): Advocates for making World Day for Cultural Diversity a national holiday.

B (Con-Team, three speakers): Argues against making it a national holiday.

  • Choose a team of judges (three students and teacher), one chairperson and one timekeeper.
  • This debate setup includes 11 active participants, while the rest of the students will be the audience who will consider both teams' arguments and vote at the end of class.
  • Judges and audience members should take notes during the debate. (Appendix 3 and 4).

‍

3. Research and Preparation (10 minutes)

‍

  1. The teacher gives each group questions to guide them through their arguments: Team A: Why is it important to have a national holiday for cultural diversity? What benefits would a national holiday bring?

Team B: What are the challenges or disadvantages of making this day a national holiday? Are there better ways to promote cultural awareness?

  • Students receive debate materials that include arguments for both sides (Appendix 2).

‍

4. The Debate (15 minutes)

‍

  • Opening statements: (6 minutes):

Team A (first speaker): Present their arguments (3 minutes). Team B (first speaker): Present their arguments (3 minutes).

  • Rebuttal round: (4 minutes):

Team A (second speaker): Responds to the Team's B arguments (2 minutes). Team B (second speaker): Responds to the Team's A arguments (2 minutes).

‍

  • Closing Statements: (4 minutes):

Team A (third speaker): Team gives their final statement (2 minutes). Team B (third speaker): Team gives their final statement (2 minutes).

‍

5. Class Reflection and Vote (5 minutes)

‍

  • After the debate, the judges will evaluate the teams' performance and explain their scores. (Appendix 3).
  • The audience will vote on the motions presented by the teams (Appendix 4).
  • Ask students to reflect on the arguments made during the debate and vote based on what they think is best for society.
  • Discuss what went well, what could have been improved, and how the arguments could be strengthened in the future.

‍

6. Conclusion (5 minutes)

‍

  • The teacher will highlight the key points that were discussed during the debate with the students.
  • Emphasize the importance of understanding different perspectives and appreciating diversity.
  • Encourage students to think about how they can contribute to the promotion of cultural diversity in their communities.
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

  • Students will understand the significance of World Day for Cultural Diversity for Dialogue and Development (May 21).
  • Students will engage in a structured debate to determine whether the day should be recognized as a national holiday.
  • Students will practice research, argumentation, public speaking and critical thinking skills.

‍

Timeline

‍

  1. Introduction to the Topic (5 minutes)
  2. Forming Debate Teams (5 minutes)
  3. Research and Preparation (10 minutes)
  4. The Debate (15 minutes)
  5. Class Reflection and Vote (5 minutes)
  6. Conclusion (4 minutes)
fields.content-03:
Learning outcomes

‍

  • Students will explore the benefits and challenges of promoting cultural diversity.
  • Students will learn how to research World Day for Cultural Diversity for Dialogue and Development and its history.
  • Students will improve their ability to present arguments and defend their views effectively.
  • Students will develop greater respect for different cultures and customs within their society.
  • Students will see that recognizing cultural diversity reduces discrimination and promotes social harmony.
fields.content-04:
Materials and tools

‍

  • Whiteboard and markers
  • Projector or computer (optional, for presenting relevant information)
  • Handout with basic facts on World Day for Cultural Diversity (overview, history, significance) or links to relevant websites
  • Appendix 1 Guidelines for the debate
  • Appendix 2 Material for teams
  • Appendix 3: Worksheet for judges.
  • Appendix 4: Worksheet for audience.
fields.content-05:
Tips and tricks

‍

  • By employing some strategies teacher can create an environment where debates are a valuable learning experience.
  • The teacher should establish guidelines for the debate (structure, timing and expectations), provide support, and offer guidance on research.
  • When classes are larger than 15 students, the teacher should assign different roles to students so that everyone can participate. This means changing the teams, judges, and audience for each debate class.
  • If the class is large, students who are not directly participating in the debate should evaluate the teams’ arguments as an audience. Digital tools can be used, where the audience will vote or ask questions after the debate, or teacher-prepared evaluation sheets. This keeps the energy level high and includes everyone.
  • Collect feedback from students on what went well and what needs improvement. Student self-evaluation can help improve the debate process for future lessons.
  • Links and literature: https://www.un.org/en/observances/cultural-diversity-day , https://www.unesco.org/en/days/cultural-diversity-dialogue-development, https://teachjustnow.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/CRO-12.-Using-Debate-Method-in-the-Classroom.pdf, https://www.living-democracy.com
fields.extra-text:
Appendix 1: Guidelines for the debate

‍

  • Time Limits: Opening statements (3 minutes each), Rebuttals (2 minutes each), Closing statements (2 minutes each).
  • Respectful Interaction: No personal attacks, only arguments should be critiqued.
  • Evidence-Based: All claims must be backed by credible evidence.
  • Speaking Order: Team A speaks first for the opening, followed by Team B. Rebuttals alternate, and closing statements end the debate.
  • Audience Role: The audience should listen attentively and take notes.
  • Students should be encouraged to use facts, examples, and logic to support their arguments.
  • Seating arrangement: The chairperson sits at the head of the table. The two debating teams are seated opposite each other. The audience sit a short distance away from the table, and should be able to see both parties. (Text and image from https://www.living-democracy.com/textbooks 

‍

‍

Appendix 2: Material for teams

‍

‍

‍Potential arguments for A team:‍

  1. Honors and encourages cultural variety.
    Celebrating the World Day of Cultural Diversity as a national holiday would encourage reflection on the values of different traditions and cultures, as well as on the importance of inclusion. This holiday could build a society that respects and values the richness of cultural diversity.
  1. Encourages national unity and social cohesion.
    As a national holiday, the World Day of Cultural Diversity has the potential to play a significant role in fostering national unity and social cohesion. Celebrating a country's rich and diverse cultural background, this holiday can help create a sense of belonging for all citizens, regardless of their cultural, ethnic or religious background.
  1. Aligns with global efforts to foster understanding and sustainable development.
    This alignment can be done through national celebrations, educational programs and initiatives that contribute to intercultural understanding and the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This holiday would contribute to creating a more inclusive, just, peaceful, and sustainable world.
  1. Provides an opportunity for cultural education.
    Cultural education helps foster greater understanding, respect and appreciation of the different cultures that make up society, and can play a key role in addressing issues such as prejudice, discrimination and social division.

‍

Potential arguments for B team:

  1. The potential economic cost of a new national holiday.
    During national holidays, businesses and government offices are usually closed, resulting in reduced productivity. Some sectors, such as retail, catering, and manufacturing, may experience financial losses. The introduction of a national holiday requires legal, administrative and logistical work to pass the necessary laws.
  1. Too many holidays may dilute their significance.
    Although holidays are an important part of cultural, economic and social life, too many of them can lead to loss of meaning, economic inefficiency and public fatigue. In order for the holidays to have value and purpose, it is important to strike a balance.
  1. Existing holidays already promote cultural understanding.
    Existing holidays already provide rich opportunities for promoting cultural understanding. Whether celebrating different customs, marking important historical events or encouraging social dialogue, holidays play a key role in educating the public, uniting people and fostering a deeper appreciation of diversity.
  1. Cultural diversity can be celebrated in other ways, such as through festivals or educational programs.
    Cultural diversity can be celebrated and promoted in a variety of ways beyond national holidays, including cultural, music and dance festivals and various educational programs. These methods can be more dynamic, inclusive and sustainable than the introduction of a national holiday.

‍
